questions.prompt: Why are OAuth grants a particularly persistent attacker foothold?
choices: They survive password resets and session revocations.

choices.label: They include the user's password in plaintext.
choices.rationale: Tokens do not carry passwords.

choices.label: They cannot be revoked once granted.
choices.rationale: They can and must be revoked. That is lesson 12's playbook step.

choices.label: They bypass MFA entirely on every sign-in.
choices.rationale: Wrong framing. Once granted, the token authenticates the app without MFA by OAuth design, not bypass.

questions.explanation: The OAuth token keeps the attacker's app authenticated even after those steps. Without explicit grant revocation, the attacker's app keeps reading mail, sending as the user, and downloading files.

questions.prompt: What are the two main attacker abuse shapes for OAuth grants?

choices.label: Steal an OAuth token from a legitimate app and reuse it.
choices.rationale: Token theft is a different mechanism. Not the lesson's named pair.

choices: Trick the user into granting via a phishing consent page.

choices.label: Brute-force the OAuth consent screen.
choices.rationale: Not how OAuth consent works.

choices.label: Create a tenant-wide policy that grants all apps.
choices.rationale: Tenant-wide policies are admin territory, not the abuse shapes in this lesson.

questions.explanation: Or grant a malicious app from inside an already-compromised account. Both shapes appear. The second (grant from inside a compromised account) is the most common one the SOC catches.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest compromise marker on an OAuth grant?

choices.label: A grant to a verified Microsoft Graph API app.
choices.rationale: Legitimate platform infrastructure, not a compromise marker.

choices.label: A grant authorised more than six months ago.
choices.rationale: Long-standing grants are typically legitimate.

choices: A new grant authorised within minutes of a suspicious sign-in.

choices.label: A grant with read-only Calendar scope.
choices.rationale: Narrow scope used by many legitimate integrations.

questions.explanation: To an app with a generic display name and broad scopes from an unverified publisher. Compromise-window timing, deceptive name, broad scopes, and unverified publisher together form the compromise marker set.

questions.prompt: "Password reset and session revoke is enough." Why is this often wrong when OAuth is involved?

choices.label: OAuth grants are stored on the user's endpoint, not the identity provider.
choices.rationale: Wrong on OAuth architecture.

choices.label: Password reset does not invalidate the old password until next sign-in.
choices.rationale: Reset invalidates immediately.

choices.label: Session revoke takes 24 hours to propagate.
choices.rationale: Revoke is near-immediate.

choices: OAuth grants survive both password reset and session revoke.
questions.explanation: The grant has to be revoked explicitly. The grant needs its own revoke step. Without it, the attacker keeps access days after you thought the compromise was fixed.

questions.prompt: "Revoking all OAuth grants is the safe response." Why is this wrong?
choices: Disrupts legitimate integrations the user relies on.

choices.label: Blanket revoke is forbidden by the identity provider.
choices.rationale: Not a platform constraint.

choices.label: It triggers an automatic tenant-wide alert.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

choices.label: Blanket revoke is impossible to perform.
choices.rationale: It is possible, just disruptive and wrong as a default.

questions.explanation: The discipline is targeted revoke of the attacker-pattern grants, not blanket revoke. Targeted revoke: the grants added during the compromise window, not all grants.

questions.prompt: What is the documented playbook order for a compromise involving an OAuth grant?

choices.label: Revoke OAuth grant first because it is the most persistent foothold.
choices.rationale: Defensible logic, wrong order. Playbook order is deliberate.

choices: Session revoke, password and MFA reset, inbox-rule remove, OAuth-grant revoke.

choices.label: Revoke the OAuth grant and skip the rest.
choices.rationale: Every other compromise artefact stays in place.

choices.label: OAuth-grant revoke, then password reset only.
choices.rationale: Skips session revoke, MFA reset, and inbox-rule removal.

questions.explanation: The playbook order minimises the time the attacker can act during the response itself.

questions.prompt: While revoking grants, you find a second grant from two weeks ago to a verified-publisher app with narrow scope. The Recommendation did not mention it. What do you do?
choices: Leave it alone; revoke only the grant named in the Recommendation.

choices.label: Revoke it as well to be thorough.
choices.rationale: Acting on context outside the Recommendation. The grant is outside the compromise window from a verified publisher.

choices.label: Message the user asking if they use the app.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex during active incident. Ad-hoc user contact about a non-compromise-related grant is not right now.

questions.explanation: Outside-window grant from a verified publisher is legitimate-by-context. Targeted-revoke discipline applies.

questions.prompt: Which set of markers indicates a legitimate OAuth grant?

choices.label: Broad scopes like Mail.ReadWrite.All.
choices.rationale: Compromise marker or admin-broad-scope.

choices.label: Generic display name like Office365Helper.
choices.rationale: Compromise marker.

choices.label: Granted in the same minute as a suspicious sign-in.
choices.rationale: Compromise marker.

questions.explanation: Scope appropriate to the app's function, user can articulate why they granted. Verified publisher, recognisable name, appropriate scope, and user awareness together form the legitimate-grant marker set.

questions.prompt: "OAuth grants are visible to the user, so they would notice." Why is this wrong?

choices.label: OAuth grants are hidden from users entirely.
choices.rationale: They are visible. Users just do not look.

choices: Most users never look at their OAuth grants.

choices.label: Users receive an email every time the grant is used.
choices.rationale: Not the default platform behaviour.

choices.label: Users have to log in to a separate portal to see grants.
choices.rationale: Grants are visible in the user's account-settings page.

questions.explanation: Attackers exploit this. The grant sits there persistently while the attacker uses it. User-visibility does not mean user-noticing. The attacker counts on the user never checking.

questions.prompt: What is the 'compromise window' in the context of OAuth-grant revocation?

choices.label: The user's working hours during the day of the compromise.
choices.rationale: Wrong framing.

choices.label: The 24-hour period after any ITDR incident lands.
choices.rationale: The window is the attacker-active window from the Timeline, not a fixed clock.

choices: The timeframe from the suspicious sign-in to the completion of session revoke.

choices.label: Any time before the password reset.
choices.rationale: Over-broad. The window has a start (suspicious sign-in) and an end (containment).

questions.explanation: Grants inside that window are suspect. Grants outside the window from verified publishers are legitimate-by-context. Grants inside the window are suspect.
